volumeTitle: Casina. The Casket Comedy. Curculio. Epidicus. The Two Menaechmuses
label: The Two Menaechmuses (Section 5.6.986 - Section 5.9.1068b)

sections.label: Section 5.6.986
sections.original: postquam ín tabernam vása et servos cónlocavi , ut iusserat ,
sections.translation: After I had deposited the goods and the servants in the inn , as he ordered me , thus am I come to meet him . ( Going to the door of EROTIUM ’ S house .) Now I ’ ll knock at the door , that he may know that I ’ m here , and that out of this thick wood [*] of peril I may get my master safe out of doors . But I ’ m afraid that I ’ m come too late , after the battle has been fought .

sections.label: Section 5.7.990
sections.original: Pér ego vobis deos atque homines dico , ut imperium meum
sections.translation: By Gods and men , I tell you prudently to pay regard to my commands , as to what I have commanded and do command . Take care that this person is carried at once upon your shoulders to the surgery , unless , indeed , you set no value upon your legs or your sides . Take care each of you to regard at a straw whatever threats he shall utter .

sections.label: Section 5.7.995
sections.original: quid státis ? quid dubitátis ? iam sublímen raptum opórtuit .
sections.translation: What are you standing for ? Why are you hesitating ? By this you ought to have had him carried off on your shoulders . I ’ ll go to the Doctor ; I ’ ll be there ready when you shall come . ( Exit . The SERVANTS gather around MENAECHMUS .)

sections.label: Section 5.7.996b
sections.original: Occidi ,
sections.translation: I ’ m undone . What business is this ? Why are these men running towards me , pray ? What do you want ? What do you seek ? Why do you stand around me ? ( They seize and drag him .) Whither are you dragging me ? Whither are you carrying me ? I ’ m undone . I entreat your assistance ,

sections.label: Section 5.7.1000
sections.original: Epidamnienses , subvenite , cives . quin me mittitis ?
sections.translation: citizens , men of Epidamnus , come and help me . ( To the men .) Why don ’ t you let me go ?

sections.label: Section 5.7.1001
sections.original: Pro di immortales , obsecro , quid ego oculis aspicio meis ?
sections.translation: O ye immortal Gods , I beseech you , what do I behold with my eyes ? Some fellows , I know not who , are most disgracefully carrying off my master upon their shoulders .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1003
sections.original: Ecquis suppetias mihi audet ferre ?
sections.translation: Who is it that ventures to bring me aid ?

sections.label: Section 5.7.1003b
sections.original: Ego , ere , audacissime .
sections.translation: I , master , and right boldly . ( Aloud .) O shameful and scandalous deed , citizens of Epidamnus , for my master ,

sections.label: Section 5.7.1005
sections.original: meum hic in pacato oppido luci deripier in via ,
sections.translation: here in a town enjoying peace , to be carried off , in daylight , in the street , who came to you a free man . Let him go .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1007b
sections.original: Obsecro te , quisquis es , operam mihi ut des ,
sections.translation: Prithee , whoever you are , do lend me your aid , and don ’ t suffer so great an outrage to be signally committed against me .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1009
sections.original: Immo et operam dabo et defendam et subvenibo sedulo .
sections.translation: Aye , I ’ ll give you my aid , and I ’ ll defend you , and zealously succour you . I ’ ll never let you come to harm ; ’ tis fitter that I myself should come to harm .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1010
sections.original: numquam te patiar perire , me perirest aequius .
sections.translation: I ’ ll now make a sowing on the faces of these fellows , and there I ’ ll plant my fists . I ’ faith , you ’ re carrying this person off this day at your own extreme hazard . Let him go . ( He lays about him .)

sections.label: Section 5.7.1014
sections.original: Teneo ego huic oculum .
sections.translation: I ’ ve got hold of this fellow ’ s eye .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1014b
sections.original: Face ut oculi locus in capite appareat .
sections.translation: Make the socket of his eye be seen in his head .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1015
sections.original: vos scelesti , vos rapacis , vos praedones .
sections.translation: You rascals ! you villains ! you robbers !

sections.label: Section 5.7.1015b
sections.original: Periimus .
sections.translation: We are undone . Troth , now , prithee , do —

sections.label: Section 5.7.1016b
sections.original: Mittite ergo .
sections.translation: Let him go then .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1016c
sections.original: Quid me vobis tactiost ?
sections.translation: What business have you to touch me ? Thump them with your fists .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1017b
sections.original: Agite abite , fugite hinc in malam crucem .
sections.translation: Come , begone , fly hence to utter perdition with you . ( Three run away .) Here ’ s for you , too ( giving the fourth one a punch ) ; because you are the last to yield , you shall have this for a reward . ( They all disappear .) Right well have I marked his face , and quite to my liking .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1020
sections.original: edepol , ere , ne tibi suppetias temperi adveni modo .
sections.translation: Troth , now , master , I really did come to your help just now in the nick of time .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1021
sections.original: At tibi di semper , adulescens , quisquis es , faciant bene .
sections.translation: And may the Gods , young man , whoever you are , ever bless you . For , had it not been for you , I should never have survived this day until sunset .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1023
sections.original: Ergo edepol , si recte facias , ere , med emittas manu .
sections.translation: By my troth , then , master , if you do right , you will give me my freedom .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1024
sections.original: Liberem ego te ?
sections.translation: I , give you your freedom ?

sections.label: Section 5.7.1024b
sections.original: Verum , quandoquidem , ere , te servavi .
sections.translation: Doubtless : since , master , I have saved you .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1024c
sections.original: Quid est ?
sections.translation: How ’ s this ?

sections.label: Section 5.7.1025
sections.original: adulescens , erras .
sections.translation: Young man , you are mistaken .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1025b
sections.original: Quid , erro ?
sections.translation: How , mistaken ?

sections.label: Section 5.7.1025c
sections.original: Per Iovem adiuro patrem ,
sections.translation: By father Jove , I solemnly swear that I am not your master .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1026b
sections.original: Non taces ?
sections.translation: Will you not hold your peace ?

sections.label: Section 5.7.1026c
sections.original: Non mentior ;
sections.translation: I ’ m telling no lie ; nor did any servant of mine ever do such a thing as you have done for me .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1028
sections.original: Sic sine igitur , si tuom négas me ésse , abire liberum .
sections.translation: In that case , then , let me go free , if you deny that I am your servant .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1029
sections.original: Mea quidem hercle causa liber esto atque ito quo voles .
sections.translation: By my faith , so far , indeed , as I ’ m concerned , be free , and go where you like .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1030
sections.original: Nempe iubes ?
sections.translation: That is , you order me to do so ?

sections.label: Section 5.7.1030b
sections.original: Iubeo hercle , si quid imperi est in te mihi .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I do order you , if I have aught of authority over you .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1031
sections.original: Salve , mi patrone . cum tu liber es , Messenio ,
sections.translation: Save you , my patron . Since you seriously give me my freedom ,

sections.label: Section 5.7.1032
sections.original: gaudeo . credo hercle vobis . sed , patrone , te obsecro ,
sections.translation: I rejoice .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1033
sections.original: ne minus imperes mihí quam cum tuos servos fui .
sections.translation: But , my patron , I do entreat you [*] that you won ’ t command me any the less now than when I was your servant . With you will I dwell , and when you go I ’ ll go home together with you . Wait for me here ;

sections.label: Section 5.7.1035b
sections.original: Nunc ibo in tabernam , vasa atque argentum tibi
sections.translation: I ’ ll now go to the inn , and bring back the luggage and the money for you . The purse , with the money for our journey , is fast sealed up in the wallet ; I ’ ll bring it just now here to you .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1037b
sections.original: Adfer strenue .
sections.translation: Bring it carefully .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1038
sections.original: Salvom tibi ita ut mihi dedisti reddibo . hic me mane .—
sections.translation: I ’ ll give it back safe to you just as you gave it to me . Do you wait for me here .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1039
sections.original: Nimia mira mihi quidem hodie exorta sunt miris modis :
sections.translation: Very wonderful things have really happened this day to me in wonderful ways .

sections.label: Section 5.7.1040
sections.original: alii me negant eum esse qui sum , atque excludunt foras
sections.translation: Some deny that I am he who I am , and shut me out of doors ; others say that I am he who I am not , and will have it that they are my servants . He for instance , who said that he was going for the money , to whom I gave his freedom just now . Since he says that he will bring me a purse with money , if he does bring it [*] , I ’ ll say that he may go free from me where he pleases ,

sections.label: Section 5.7.1045
sections.original: ne tum , quando sanus factus sit , a me argentum petat .
sections.translation: lest at a time when he shall have come to his senses he should ask the money of me . My father-in-law and the Doctor were saying that I am mad . Whatever it is , it is a wonderful affair . These things appear to me not at all otherwise than dreams . Now I ’ ll go in the house to this Courtesan , although she is angry with me ; if I can prevail upon her to restore the mantle for me to take back home . ( He goes into EROTIUM ’ S house .)

sections.label: Section 5.8.1050
sections.original: Men hodie usquam convenisse te , audax , audes dicere ,
sections.translation: Do you dare affirm , audacious fellow , that I have ever met you this day since the time when I ordered you to come here to meet me ?

sections.label: Section 5.8.1051b
sections.original: Quin modo
sections.translation: Why , I just now rescued you before this house , when four men were carrying you off upon their shoulders . You invoked the aid of all Gods and men , when I ran up and delivered you by main force , fighting , and in spite of them .

sections.label: Section 5.8.1055
sections.original: ob eam rem , quia te servavi , me amisisti liberum .
sections.translation: For this reason , because I rescued you , you set me at liberty . When I said that I was going for the money and the luggage , you ran before to meet me as quickly as you could , in order that you might deny what you did .

sections.label: Section 5.8.1058
sections.original: Liberum ego te iussi abire ?
sections.translation: I , bade you go away a free man ?

sections.label: Section 5.8.1058b
sections.original: Certo .
sections.translation: Certainly .

sections.label: Section 5.8.1058c
sections.original: Quin certissimumst ,
sections.translation: Why , on the contrary , ’ tis most certain that I myself would rather become a slave than ever give you your freedom .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1060
sections.original: Si vóltis per oculos iurare , nihilo hercle ea causa magis
sections.translation: If you are ready to swear by your eyes , by my troth , not a bit the more for that reason , most vile woman , will you make it that I took away the mantle and the bracelet to-day .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1062
sections.original: Pro di ímmortales , quid ego video ?
sections.translation: Immortal Gods , what do I see ?

sections.label: Section 5.9.1062b
sections.original: Quid vides ?
sections.translation: What do you see ?

sections.label: Section 5.9.1062c
sections.original: Speculum tuom .
sections.translation: Your resemblance in a mirror .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1063
sections.original: Quid negoti est ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 5.9.1063b
sections.original: Tuast imago . tam consimilest quam potest .
sections.translation: ’ Tis your image ; ’ tis as like as possible .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1064
sections.original: Pol profecto haud est dissimilis , meam quom formam noscito .
sections.translation: Troth , it really is not unlike , so far as I know my own form .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1065
sections.original: O adulescens , salve , qui me servavisti , quisquis es .
sections.translation: O young man , save you , you who preserved me , whoever you are .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1066
sections.original: Adulescens , quaeso hercle eloquere tuom mihi nomen , nisi piget .
sections.translation: By my troth , young man , prithee , tell me your name , unless it ’ s disagreable .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1067
sections.original: Non edepol ita promeruisti de me , ut pigeat , quae velis
sections.translation: I ’ faith , you ’ ve not so deserved of me , that it should be disagreable for me to tell what you wish . My name is Menaechmus .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1068b
sections.original: Immo edepol mihi .
sections.translation: Why , by my troth , so is mine .
